description: The currently displayed image is an irrelevant placeholder interface button marked with the text "Check for updates", which is completely inconsistent with the provided research context of hypereutectic TiB₂ reinforced low-density high-modulus steel. No valid materials science-related academic figure content, including microstructure characterization results, mechanical property test curves, finite element simulation outputs, or phase structure diagrams, can be extracted from this non-scientific interface icon, so the target figure information for the steel research cannot be identified or analyzed based on the current provided visual content.

This figure contains two subgraphs for alloy design and processing route illustration of the novel low-density high-modulus Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel. Subgraph (a) shows equilibrium phase volume fractions as a function of temperature from 600 °C to 1500 °C, calculated via Thermo-Calc with TCFE9 database for the nominal Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B (wt.%) alloy, covering four phases: Liquid (red curve), Ferrite (grey curve), Austenite (blue curve) and TiB₂ (golden curve). TiB₂ maintains a stable volume fraction of ~0.125 over a wide temperature range, austenite dominates at 700~1150 °C, ferrite exists mainly at 1000~1400 °C, and liquid phase forms above 1200 °C. Subgraph (b) is the schematic fabrication route: vacuum induction melting of raw materials, casting into ingot, forging and homogenization, then hot rolling to thin sheet. This figure verifies that the designed alloy can achieve the target triplex microstructure (austenite + ferrite + ~12.5 vol.% TiB₂) via the designed conventional metallurgical process, supporting the subsequent development of low-density high-modulus steel.

This figure presents the finite element simulation setups for the low-density high-modulus TiB₂ reinforced Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel, which consists of austenite, δ-ferrite, and TiB₂ phases. Subplot (a) shows the EBSD-based 200 μm × 200 μm representative volume element (RVE) with periodic boundary conditions, marking up/down/left/right edges, reference point RP-1 for loading and RP-2 for fixation. Subplot (b) displays the 2D finite element model distinguishing different phase layers: γ-Fe, γ-Fe/TiB₂ phase boundary, TiB₂, δ-Fe/TiB₂ phase boundary, and δ-Fe, with the color legend: blue for austenite, red for δ-ferrite, yellow for TiB₂. Subplot (c) provides a close-up of local finite element meshes, which adopts a 0.4 μm mesh size corresponding to 500×500 elements to improve simulation accuracy. This RVE setup is designed to correlate the microstructure and mechanical properties of the studied steel, and its validity is confirmed by matching simulation results with experimental mechanical responses.

This figure presents mechanical and lightweight-related performance of the newly developed low-density steel (Our LDS, nominal composition Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B) in comparison with reference steels and other reported lightweight steels produced via conventional metallurgy. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves: Our LDS exhibits yield strength of 503 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 672 MPa, and uniform elongation of 13%, outperforming reference Fe-TiB₂ steel with higher strength and better ductility, and interstitial-free steel with far higher strength. Subplot (b) is a property map of uniform elongation versus specific yield strength, where Our LDS locates at a position with outstanding strength-ductility synergy among all compared lightweight steels. Subplot (c) compares specific modulus and the product of specific yield strength and uniform elongation, showing Our LDS achieves a high specific modulus of 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³ and excellent SYS×UE value, outperforming most existing lightweight steels, confirming its superior comprehensive performance for lightweight structural applications.

This figure characterizes the initial microstructure and TiB₂ particle size distribution of the hypereutectic TiB₂ reinforced low-density high-modulus steel (Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B wt.%, processed by vacuum melting, homogenization and hot rolling). Subfigure (a) is a pseudo 3D SEM image showing primary and eutectic TiB₂ particles on surfaces along three directions; (b) and (c) are high-magnification SEM images presenting TiB₂ morphology and distinguishing Mn-rich austenite and Al-rich δ-ferrite regions, respectively. (d) is XRD pattern confirming three phases of δ-ferrite, austenite and TiB₂. (e) shows micro-CT measured spatial distribution of TiB₂ particles in different size domains, while (f) and (g) are statistical histograms of particle number and volume fractions separately: 0.8-5 μm particles occupy the largest number fraction (~46%), while 10-20 μm and 20-42.7 μm particles account for ~56% and ~32% of total volume fraction respectively, revealing coarse TiB₂ particles dominate the total volume of reinforcements and thus dictate the bulk mechanical performance of the steel.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of the initial microstructure of the hot-rolled low-density high-modulus TiB₂-reinforced steel (Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B, wt.%). It includes pseudo-3D inverse pole figure (IPF) map (a), phase map (a₁), kernel average misorientation (KAM) map (a₂), as well as corresponding 2D IPF (b), phase (b₁), KAM (b₂) maps, grain size distribution (b₃), overall grain orientation spread (GOS) map (c), austenite GOS map (c₁), δ-ferrite GOS map (c₂), and GOS frequency distribution (c₃). The phase map confirms three phases: blue austenite (γ, average grain size 3.9 μm), red δ-ferrite (average grain size 4.4 μm), yellow TiB₂ particles, with a banded layered structure of alternating δ-ferrite and austenite parallel to the rolling direction (RD). KAM maps show higher local strain accumulation in δ-ferrite. GOS analysis reveals 90.8% of austenite grains are fully recrystallized (GOS < 2°), while only 52.2% of δ-ferrite grains are recrystallized, indicating slower recrystallization kinetics of δ-ferrite during hot rolling. Scale bars are 50 μm for 3D maps and 20 μm for 2D maps, with directions defined as rolling direction (RD) and transverse direction (TD).

This set of images presents TEM characterizations of dislocation morphologies in the hot-rolled microstructure of the low-density high-modulus TiB₂-reinforced steel. Subfigure (a) is a bright-field (BF) TEM image of BCC δ-ferrite grains distant from TiB₂ particles, with inset selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) of BCC <001> zone axis, showing dense dislocation tangles. Subfigure (a₁) is a BF-TEM image of δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface, showing dislocation pile-ups, with inset SADP of BCC <111> zone axis. Subfigure (a₂) is a weak-beam BF-TEM image of the same δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface, with inset SADP marked g=101, showing dislocation tangles at the interface. Subfigure (b) is a BF-TEM image of FCC austenite grains distant from TiB₂ particles, with inset SADP of FCC <011> zone axis, showing sparse dislocation tangles. Subfigure (b₁) is a BF-TEM image of austenite/TiB₂ interface marked by blue line, showing dislocation pile-ups. Subfigure (b₂) is a weak-beam BF-TEM image of the same austenite/TiB₂ interface, with inset SADP marked g=11̅1, showing sparse dislocation tangles. The comparison reveals δ-ferrite has significantly higher dislocation density than austenite, and dislocation density at δ-ferrite/TiB₂ interface is much higher than that at austenite/TiB₂ interface, supporting the conclusion that dislocation interaction differs between the two matrix phases and TiB₂ reinforcement after hot rolling, with higher initial dislocation density in δ-ferrite.

This figure shows EBSD and TEM characterization results of the developed low-density TiB2-reinforced high Al ferrite steel after tensile straining. Pseudo-3D EBSD maps are provided in (a) to (a2): (a) is the inverse pole figure (IPF) map for grain orientation characterization, (a1) is the phase map marking FCC austenite (blue), BCC δ-ferrite (red) and TiB2 (yellow), (a2) is the kernel average misorientation (KAM) map showing local strain distribution, all with 50 μm scale bars. Subfigures (b) to (b2) display TEM bright-field images of δ-ferrite: (b) shows δ-ferrite grains far from TiB2 with BCC <110> selected area electron diffraction (SAED) inset, (b1) exhibits severe dislocation pile-ups and tangles at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interface, (b2) is the corresponding SAED of the interface. Subfigures (c) to (c2) are TEM bright-field images of austenite: (c) is austenite grains away from TiB2 with FCC <001> SAED inset, (c1) shows sparse dislocation pile-ups at austenite/TiB2 interface, (c2) is the corresponding SAED. This figure verifies the distinct dislocation activities in two matrix phases, supporting the synergistic strengthening and toughening mechanism of the steel, where δ-ferrite provides stable strengthening and austenite contributes extra work hardening for good ductility.

This figure characterizes the tensile damage behavior of the developed low-density high-modulus TiB₂-reinforced Fe-Mn-Al steel consisting of FCC austenite, BCC δ-ferrite, and TiB₂ particles. Pseudo-in-situ SEM images (a-a₃) capture microstructure evolution under 0%, 0.7%, 5%, 9% room-temperature engineering tensile strain, with red arrows marking TiB₂ particle cracking and blue arrows marking particle/matrix decohesion. Statistical plots (a₄,a₅) quantify TiB₂ crack count, decohesion count and their relative frequencies, showing higher damage occurrence for TiB₂ adjacent to FCC austenite than BCC ferrite as strain rises. Multi-scale SEM images (b-b₂) reveal crack propagation pathways near fracture surface, highlighting large TiB₂ fracture, interfacial decohesion and cluster damage. Fractographs (c,c₁) confirm a mixed fracture mode with ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage TiB₂ fracture and interfacial decohesion. EDS elemental maps verify Fe/Mn/Al enrich in the matrix, while Ti/B correspond to TiB₂ particles. It supports the conclusion that tensile damage arises from both TiB₂ fracture and interfacial decohesion, with TiB₂ surrounded by austenite having higher damage susceptibility.

This figure presents local von Mises strain evolution in a hypereutectic TiB₂ reinforced low-density steel (Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B, consisting of austenite (γ), δ-ferrite, and TiB₂ phases) under tensile loading along the rolling direction at different macro strain levels. Subfigure (a) is the 0% strain EBSD phase map showing the initial tri-phase microstructure with a 50 μm scale bar. Subfigures (b-f) display strain maps at 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, 15% macro strain respectively (20 μm scale bar, von Mises strain ranges 0 to 0.20), revealing that strain concentrations preferentially localize at TiB₂/matrix interfaces ~45° to the loading axis as applied strain increases. Subfigures (g-i) plot local strain profiles along 3 paths marked in (a)'s inset: (g) across small TiB₂/δ-ferrite boundaries, (h) across interfaces of differently sized TiB₂ particles and the matrix, (i) across sharp TiB₂/γ, TiB₂/δ-ferrite and γ/δ-ferrite interfaces. The profiles confirm peak local strains up to ~20% at phase boundaries, higher local strain in softer austenite than δ-ferrite, and promoted plastic deformation in the matrix adjacent to TiB₂ particles, supporting that strain partitioning at heterogeneous interfaces contributes to the steel's balanced strength and ductility.

This figure presents synchrotron X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis results of a low-density Fe-16Mn-3.9Al-5.11Ti-2.09B steel consisting of 62 vol.% austenite, 26 vol.% δ-ferrite, and 12.5 vol.% TiB₂. Subfigures (a) and (b) show XRD line profiles (x-axis: K, the reciprocal of lattice spacing, unit nm⁻¹; y-axis: intensity in a.u.) for the undeformed sample and the sample after tensile fracture, respectively, with peaks indexed to δ-ferrite (black labels), austenite (blue labels), and TiB₂ (red labels). Subfigures (c) and (d) are Modified Williamson–Hall (MWH) plots for δ-ferrite and austenite, respectively (x-axis: KC^(1/2) in nm⁻¹; y-axis: ΔK in nm⁻¹), where solid points represent undeformed samples, hollow points represent deformed samples, and linear fits are used to calculate dislocation density. Subfigure (e) is a bar chart of dislocation density (unit: 10¹⁵ m⁻²) for both phases before and after deformation, with blue bars for austenite and red bars for δ-ferrite. Key findings: The initial dislocation density of δ-ferrite (1.1±0.2×10¹⁵ m⁻²) is higher than that of austenite (3±0.7×10¹⁴ m⁻²). After deformation, the dislocation density of austenite significantly increases to 2±0.3×10¹⁵ m⁻², while that of δ-ferrite only slightly rises to 1.7±0.7×10¹⁵ m⁻², indicating austenite dominates the work hardening during plastic deformation. This figure validates the quantitative characterization of dislocation density evolution in dual-phase steels via synchrotron XRD.

This figure presents finite element simulation results of mechanical responses and microstructure evolutions for the novel low-density TiB₂-reinforced steel (Our LDS), conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel, and interstitial-free (IF) steel, validated by experimental data. Subplot (a) shows true stress-true strain curves, comparing damaged (hollow circles) and undamaged (solid lines) simulation results with experimental tensile curves: the simulated curves match experimental measurements well, and Our LDS exhibits significantly higher strength than the other two steels. Subplot (b) plots dislocation density evolution of δ-ferrite and austenite matrix phases with true strain: simulation curves align with experimental values, δ-ferrite has higher initial dislocation density, while austenite shows faster dislocation accumulation during deformation. Subplot (c) illustrates stress partitioning among phases (TiB₂, δ-ferrite, austenite) as global true stress increases: the hardest TiB₂ bears the highest stress, austenite the lowest, confirming uneven stress distribution, and revealing that austenite yields first, followed by load transfer to δ-ferrite and TiB₂ with further deformation. The figure validates the accuracy of the finite element model, and supports the strengthening mechanism of the heterogeneous triplex microstructure of the developed steel.

This figure presents finite element simulation results of dislocation density increment distribution and phase contributions to overall flow stress in TiB₂-reinforced steels during tensile deformation, alongside experimental validation. Subfigures (a) and (b) show the evolution of dislocation density increment (ρ-ρ₀, ranging from 2×10¹⁴ m⁻² to 23×10¹⁴ m⁻²) at global strains of 0.0%, 5.0% and 11.0% for conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel and the developed low-density steel (LDS) respectively, with red circles marking nearly undeformed zones induced by stress shielding in conventional steel. Subfigure (c) is a true stress-true strain plot, comparing experimental tensile data of the developed LDS with simulated strengthening contributions of austenite, δ-ferrite and elastic load-bearing TiB₂ weighted by their volume fractions. Key trends reveal that the heterogeneous multi-phase structure of the developed LDS leads to more uniform dislocation density distribution, suppressed shear band formation at early deformation stages, and synergistic strengthening: δ-ferrite provides a stable ~170 MPa strength base, while austenite contributes increasing work hardening with strain. The results confirm the designed heterogeneous microstructure achieves simultaneous high strength and good ductility superior to conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel.

This figure compares experimental observations and finite element (FE) simulation results of TiB₂ particle behavior in a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-Ti-B steel (composed of austenite, δ-ferrite, and 12.5 vol.% TiB₂) during tensile deformation. At 5% global engineering strain: (a) SEM image presents fractured TiB₂ particles; (a₁) is the μ-DIC measured equivalent strain map; (a₂) is the FE simulated plastic equivalent strain (PEEQ) map; (a₃) shows σ₁₁ stress versus engineering strain curves for marked particles, capturing their fracture events. At 7% global engineering strain: (b) SEM image exhibits both particle fracture and interfacial decohesion; (b₁) is the μ-DIC strain map; (b₂) is the FE PEEQ map; (b₃) gives stress-strain curves for the decohesion of Particle 3 and two fracture events of Particle 4. (c) is a schematic illustrating microstructure evolution and deformation mechanisms, including plastic slip bands, dislocations, particle cracking voids and interfacial debonding voids. The figure confirms that agglomerated TiB₂ particles fracture preferentially, and particle fracture accelerates specimen failure more severely than interfacial decohesion, both triggered by severe local strain concentration around TiB₂ particles.
